other_observers: I believe Cole Wild and Nick Komar saw this bird briefly the evening of the 19th and perhaps at daybreak on the 20th.;
specific_location: Cottonwood Hollow Natural Area on the east side of town south of Prospect, east of the Poudre River. The exact location was the northeast corner of Artist's Point Pond.
description: The bird was a very small wader, roughly killdeer sized, although the body appeared fuller. The overall color was medium dark rusty brown with the distal half of the wings being blackish, as was the back and top of the head. It had a long bill, sharp and thin at the tip, much thicker at the base (i.e., like a heron, not a typical shorebird). The flanks were whitish. The underside of the wings did not have the pattern of the upper wing surface and appeared to be dark gray thoughout. The feet extended just beyond the tail tip. I could not discern (or do not remember) their color.Behaviors: The bird was flushed from a narrow area of dense cattails bordering open water on one side and a wide peninsula of land on the other. It flushed about 10 feet away from me, flew about 30 yards with cattail leaf tops between me and the bird, and landed again in the cattails (my glimpse was perhaps 4-5 seconds and I never saw the bird again). As reported above, the bird gave an aggitated flight call a couple times during its brief flight.Call: in flight, the bird gave a rather loud "ruck" note 2-3 times, spaced a few seconds apart during a 30 yard flightPlumage: adult?
